local_dishes.name: Tempura
local_dishes.description: Lightly battered and deep-fried seafood and vegetables, known for delicate crispiness
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Okonomiyaki
local_dishes.description: Savory pancake with cabbage
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Sushi
local_dishes.description: raw fish served on rice seasoned lightly with vinegar
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Ramen
local_dishes.description: Comforting bowl of rich, flavourful broth with perfectly cooked noodles and toppings like chicken, seafood, or egg
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Udon noodles
local_dishes.description: thick, chewy noodles traditionally made from wheat flour and brine water, served in various ways
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Gyoza
local_dishes.description: Pan-fried dumplings
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Mochi
local_dishes.description: Wonderfully chewy and stretchy, mochi rice cakes are made from pounded steamed rice and often stuffed with sweet bean paste
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Wagashi
local_dishes.description: More like works of art than food, wagashi sweets are an unmissable Japanese treat. Traditionally made with only plant-based ingredients such as bean paste, they're beautifully crafted in the shape of flowers and other stunning designs
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Miso soup
local_dishes.description: often with tofu and green onions, is part of every set meal including breakfast
source_files: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf
Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Vegetarian Ramen (Yasai Ramen)
local_dishes.description: Noodles in a flavorful vegetable or miso broth topped with corn, mushrooms, and greens

local_dishes.name: Onigiri (Rice Balls)
local_dishes.description: Rice triangles often filled with pickled plum (umeboshi) or seasoned vegetables

local_dishes.name: Vegetable Tempura
local_dishes.description: Lightly battered and fried vegetables like sweet potato, pumpkin, and eggplant

local_dishes.name: Japanese Vegetable Curry (Yasai Kare)
local_dishes.description: Mild, slightly sweet curry with potatoes, carrots, and onions served with rice

local_dishes.name: Inari Sushi
local_dishes.description: Sweet tofu pockets stuffed with vinegared rice; usually vegetarian

local_dishes.name: Edamame
local_dishes.description: Steamed young soybeans sprinkled with salt; a simple and healthy snack

local_dishes.name: Soba Noodles (Vegetarian)
local_dishes.description: Buckwheat noodles served cold with dipping sauce or hot in broth (confirm no fish stock)

local_dishes.name: Vegetable Yakitori / Kushiyaki
local_dishes.description: Grilled skewers of mushrooms, peppers, or tofu (ask for no meat/fish)

local_dishes.name: Goma-ae
local_dishes.description: Spinach or vegetables dressed in a rich sesame sauce

local_dishes.name: Shojin Ryori
local_dishes.description: Traditional Buddhist temple cuisine; fully vegetarian and beautifully presented

local_dishes.name: Matcha Desserts
local_dishes.description: Matcha parfaits, ice creams, cakes, and puddings
local_dishes.where_to_try: Kyoto Uji tea shops, Tokyo department stores

local_dishes.name: Yaki Imo
local_dishes.description: Roasted Japanese sweet potato, a popular street snack

local_dishes.name: Ukemono
local_dishes.description: Japanese pickled vegetables are an essential part of a Japanese meal and are always included in set meals

local_dishes.name: Zaru Soba
local_dishes.description: Cold soba (buckwheat) noodles are popular in the summer served on a bamboo tray with nori seaweed, spring onion, wasabi
source_files: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Nasu dengaku
local_dishes.description: This classic Japanese side dish consists of grilled eggplant cut into slices and coated with a delicious, rich miso glaze
source_files: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Veg Sushi
local_dishes.description: lots of sushi in Japan is vegetarian. Look out for treats such as kappa maki (cucumber rolls), takuan maki (pickled daikon rolls) and inarizushi (rice stuffed inside deep-fried tofu pockets)

local_dishes.name: Vegetarian onigiri
local_dishes.description: Triangular onigiri rice balls are a quintessential Japanese snack, and several of them are vegetarian. Keep an eye out for ones filled with kombu seaweed, umeboshi (pickled plum), and natto (fermented soybeans)
source_files: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Sushi & Sashimi
local_dishes.description: Fresh seafood delicacies served as nigiri (on rice) or sashimi (without rice)

local_dishes.name: Japanese Curry (Kare Raisu)
local_dishes.description: Mild, hearty curry with rice, often served with fried cutlet (katsu)

local_dishes.name: Dango & Mochi
local_dishes.description: Sweet rice dumplings on skewers, often with soy, red bean, or matcha flavors

local_dishes.name: Wagyu Beef / Kobe Beef
local_dishes.description: Melt-in-your-mouth beef, often served as steak, yakiniku, or in a hot pot
local_dishes.where_to_try: Kobe, Matsusaka, specialty restaurants in Tokyo

local_dishes.name: Unagi (Grilled Eel)
local_dishes.description: Richly flavored eel, usually served over rice

local_dishes.name: Gyoza (Japanese Dumplings)
local_dishes.description: Pan-fried or steamed dumplings with pork or veggie filling

local_dishes.name: Kakigori (Shaved Ice)
local_dishes.description: Flavored shaved ice, especially in summer

local_dishes.name: Dorayaki
local_dishes.description: Red bean-filled pancake, great snack while exploring
local_dishes.where_to_try: Local sweet shops in Tokyo or Kyoto

local_dishes.name: Sashimi
local_dishes.description: raw fish or meat expertly cut into thin slices, served with daikon radish, pickled ginger, wasabi and soy sauce
source_files: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Onigiri
local_dishes.description: Triangular rice balls, portions of Japanese rice with filling in centre, moulded into triangular or cylindrical shapes wrapped in nori seaweed
source_files: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Soba
local_dishes.description: Buckwheat noodles, delicious chilled or in a hot broth
source_files: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Curry Rice
local_dishes.description: Japanese curry that is sweeter in flavour, thicker in texture, and prepared like a stew
source_files: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Vegetarian Sushi
local_dishes.description: kappa maki (cucumber rolls), takuan maki (pickled daikon rolls) and inarizushi (rice in deep-fried tofu pockets)
source_files: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

local_dishes.name: Yakitori
local_dishes.description: Grilled skewers, primarily chicken, cooked over charcoal for smoky flavour

local_dishes.name: Japanese Curry
local_dishes.description: Comforting dish featuring mildly spiced, thick curry served with rice and choice of chicken or seafood

local_dishes.name: Udon & Soba Noodles
local_dishes.description: Traditional Japanese noodles served hot or cold—udon is thick and chewy, soba is thinner and made from buckwheat

local_dishes.name: Karaage
local_dishes.description: Crispy, juicy bite-sized chicken pieces marinated in soy, ginger, and garlic, then deep-fried

local_dishes.name: Udon
local_dishes.description: Thick wheat noodles served hot or cold, often with tempura or tofu

local_dishes.name: Matcha (Green Tea)
local_dishes.description: Often enjoyed in tea ceremonies or desserts

local_dishes.name: Mochi & Daifuku
local_dishes.description: Sticky rice cakes filled with sweet red bean paste or fruit

local_dishes.name: Sake
local_dishes.description: Traditional Japanese rice wine

safety_tips.tip: Avoid Dashi (a popular condiment used in dipping sauces) since it contains fish sauce
safety_tips._source_file: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf

safety_tips.tip: Carry your passport copy – Keep the original safe in the hotel when possible
Stay aware in crowded areas – Especially at busy stations and markets
Keep cash in small amounts – Japan is safe, but smart money handling is wise
Note last train timings – Trains stop around midnight in many cities
Follow pedestrian signals strictly – Jaywalking is frowned upon
Check food ingredients – Vegetarian items may contain fish stock (dashi)
Smoke only in designated areas – Fines may apply otherwise
Follow earthquake safety instructions – Stay calm and follow local guidance if tremors occur
Carry your passport copy – Keep the original safe in the hotel when possible

Keep cash in small amounts – Japan is safe, but smart money handling is wise
Use anti-theft bags or zipped backpacks – Prevents accidental loss
Note last train timings – Trains stop around midnight in many cities
Save emergency numbers – Police: 110, Ambulance/Fire: 119
Download offline maps – Helpful if internet drops
Check weather daily – March can bring sudden rain
Follow pedestrian signals strictly – Jaywalking is frowned upon
Use ATMs at convenience stores – Most foreign cards work there
Smoke only in designated areas – Fines may apply otherwise
Follow earthquake safety instructions – Stay calm and follow local guidance if tremors occur
Wear comfortable shoes – You will walk a lot
Japan is one of the safest countries globally, but keep valuables zipped up in crowded places like Shibuya and Dotonbori

Bar & Club Overcharging (especially in Tokyo nightlife areas) - Promoters may lure tourists into bars that later add huge hidden charges
Fake or Inflated Bills - Some small nightlife venues add unexplained service or table fees
Street Promoter Pressure - Aggressive touts may push you to enter specific clubs or massage places
Fake Closing Down Sale Shops - Rare but some souvenir shops advertise fake heavy discounts
Taxi Route Padding (very uncommon but possible) - A driver may take a longer route if they think you don't know the way
Temple/Photo Donation Pressure - Someone may offer to take your photo and then strongly ask for money
Card Skimming (rare but possible anywhere) - Risk increases at isolated ATMs or suspicious machines
Gashapon or Game Machine Tricks - Some arcade machines are set very difficult to win prizes
Fake Hotel Calls/Emails - Phishing messages pretending to be from your hotel or booking site

connectivity_tips.tip: There are 4 main SIM Card providers in Japan, but NTT Docomo is recommended for better coverage
connectivity_tips._source_file: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: The best place to buy a sim card is at the airport kiosks or at a convenience store
connectivity_tips._source_file: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: Even if you do not purchase an international roaming plan for your current India number, remember to activate roaming so that you receive messages (important for things like OTPs)
connectivity_tips._source_file: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: Major mobile networks: NTT Docomo (best nationwide coverage), SoftBank (good city speeds), au by KDDI (reliable urban), Rakuten Mobile (cheaper but weaker rural coverage)
Best tourist options: Data-Only Tourist SIM (Japan Travel SIM, Sakura Mobile, Mobal SIM) or eSIM for hassle-free setup
Popular SIM providers use Docomo network for best coverage in rural and mountainous areas
NTT Docomo is recommended for better coverage among 4 main SIM Card providers
Best place to buy a sim card is at the airport kiosks or convenience stores
Activate roaming to receive messages for OTPs even if not purchasing international roaming plan

Major Mobile Networks: NTT Docomo (best nationwide coverage), SoftBank (very good speeds in cities), au by KDDI (reliable urban coverage), Rakuten Mobile (cheaper but weaker coverage outside big cities)
Data-Only Tourist SIM: Easy to use, no Japanese phone number needed, good for maps/WhatsApp/browsing. Popular providers: Japan Travel SIM (Docomo network), Sakura Mobile, Mobal SIM
eSIM: Activate instantly, no physical SIM change, good for short trips. Requires eSIM-compatible phone
Best SIM card option is NTT Docomo for excellent coverage across Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka, and rural areas like Mt. Fuji
connectivity_tips._source_file: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: Buy SIM card at the airport (most reliable), major electronic stores like BIC Camera and Yodobashi, or select convenience stores
connectivity_tips._source_file: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: Prepaid SIMs and eSIMs available with unlimited data (usually capped at high speed after threshold), no ID needed but requires unlocked phone
connectivity_tips._source_file: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: Consider eSIM services: Airalo, Ubigi, Sakura Mobile
connectivity_tips._source_file: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: Keep Indian number active to receive OTPs and bank messages even if not using international roaming
connectivity_tips._source_file: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: Use Wi-Fi at hotels and train stations to save data
connectivity_tips._source_file: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

connectivity_tips.tip: There are 4 main SIM Card providers in Japan, but I would recommend NTT Docomo for better coverage. The best place to buy a sim card is at the airport kiosks. You can also buy it at a convenience store. Even if you do not purchase an international roaming plan for your current India number, remember to activate roaming so that you receive messages (important for things like OTPs).
Major networks include NTT Docomo (best nationwide coverage), SoftBank (very good speeds in cities), au by KDDI (reliable urban coverage), and Rakuten Mobile (cheaper but weaker outside big cities)
Data-Only Tourist SIM is most popular - easy to use, no Japanese phone number needed, good for maps and WhatsApp. Popular providers include Japan Travel SIM, Sakura Mobile, and Mobal SIM
eSIM is most convenient if phone supports it - activate instantly, no physical SIM change, good for short trips but requires eSIM-compatible phone
Prepaid SIM Cards: Widely available for tourists at airports, convenience stores, and electronics shops
Major Providers: NTT Docomo, SoftBank, au by KDDI, and Rakuten Mobile
Airport Availability: Purchase SIMs at Narita, Haneda, Kansai, and Chubu Airports upon arrival
Data Plans: Choose based on duration (7, 14, or 30 days) and data needs (1GB–20GB)
Pocket Wi-Fi: Great option for families or groups—connect multiple devices at once
Activation: Most SIMs are plug-and-play; some require online registration or passport verification
SIM Card Size: Japan supports nano, micro, and standard SIMs—check your device compatibility
Unlocked Phone Required: Ensure your phone is unlocked before travel to use local SIMs
Convenience Stores: Lawson, 7-Eleven, and FamilyMart often sell prepaid SIMs
Electronics Stores: Yodobashi Camera, BicCamera, and Don Quijote have a wide SIM selection
Free Wi-Fi Spots: Found in train stations, airports, and cafes, but can be slow or limited
Emergency Connectivity Tip: Keep offline maps and translation apps downloaded in advance
NTT Docomo – Best nationwide coverage, especially in rural and mountainous areas
SoftBank – Very good speeds in cities and tourist zones
au by KDDI – Reliable network with strong urban coverage
Rakuten Mobile – Cheaper but coverage can be weaker outside big cities
Data-Only Tourist SIM cards are most popular - easy to use, no Japanese phone number needed
Popular SIM providers: Japan Travel SIM (Docomo network), Sakura Mobile, Mobal SIM
eSIM option available for instant activation if phone supports it

Major Mobile Networks: NTT Docomo (best nationwide coverage), SoftBank (very good speeds in cities), au by KDDI (reliable urban coverage), Rakuten Mobile (cheaper but weaker coverage outside big cities)

Data-Only Tourist SIM is most popular - easy to use, no Japanese phone number needed, good for maps/WhatsApp/browsing but usually no voice calls. Popular providers: Japan Travel SIM (Docomo network), Sakura Mobile, Mobal SIM
eSIM is most convenient if phone supports it - activate instantly, no physical SIM change, good for short trips but requires eSIM-compatible phone

transport_options.description: Buy a prepaid Icoca/Suica/Pasmo card for local travel (railways/buses/subways) in all Japanese cities
transport_options.cost: ICOCA Card costs ¥2,000, which includes ¥1,500 in preloaded value and a ¥500 deposit. Typically consider 1000-2000 yen per day
transport_options._source_file: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf

transport_options.description: Rechargeable contactless smart card for trains, subways, and buses - just tap in and tap out
transport_options.recommended_pass: ICOCA (Kansai region) or Suica (Tokyo region) - interchangeable nationwide

transport_options.description: For local travel (railways/buses/subways) in all Japanese cities
transport_options.cost: ICOCA Card ¥2,000 (¥1,500 value + ¥500 refundable deposit), Suica has refundable deposit, Pasmo no refundable deposit

transport_options.description: Rechargeable contactless smart card used for trains, subways, and buses. Simply tap in and tap out at gates
Japan Railway lines connecting major cities and regions
Urban rail systems in major cities
ICOCA / Suica / Pasmo cards for public transport
transport_options.cost: Load with ¥5000 to start, pick up at metro station closest to hotel

transport_options.description: High-speed rail connecting major cities like Tokyo to Kyoto
transport_options.cost: Example: Tokyo to Kyoto takes 2 hr 51 min
transport_options._source_file: Japan/V2 - All Inclusive Guide- Japan- Sirisha.pdf

transport_options.description: Buy a prepaid Icoca/Suica/Pasmo card for local travel (railways/buses/subways) in all Japanese cities
transport_options.cost: The ICOCA Card costs ¥2,000, which includes ¥1,500 in preloaded value and a ¥500 deposit that you can get back when you return the card at the end of your trip. Suica card also has refundable deposit. Pasmo doesn't have refundable deposit. Typically consider 1000-2000 yen per day

transport_options.description: Rechargeable contactless smart card used for trains, subways, and buses. Simply tap in and tap out at gates

Bullet train for long-distance travel between cities

health_tips.tip: Travel Insurance recommended - TATA AIG
health_tips._source_file: Mauritius/All Inclusive Guide- Japan- AK and families.pdf

health_tips.tip: Vegetarian food may contain dashi (fish broth) - always confirm 'No meat, no fish, no broth'
Carry personal medications and small first-aid kit
Use ATMs at convenience stores as most foreign cards work there
Keep hand sanitizer and wet wipes for cleanliness
Travel Insurance recommended - TATA AIG
Carry doctors prescriptions/travel prescriptions for medicines (except over the counter meds)
Recommended vaccinations: Measles, Mumps, Rubella (MMR), Tetanus-Diphtheria-Pertussis (Tdap), Polio, Influenza (seasonal flu vaccine recommended in winter/spring)
Hepatitis A recommended if you plan to eat street food or visit rural areas
Hepatitis B recommended if you may have medical procedures or close contact with locals
Japanese Encephalitis optional; mainly for long-term stays in rural or forested areas
Carry insect repellent for rural areas or parks
Tap water is safe in Japan; eat freshly prepared food, especially sushi or street snacks
Travel Insurance strongly recommended — includes medical coverage and emergencies
Bring necessary prescriptions and a copy of the prescription
Use sunscreen and hats for day trips, especially in April
Consult your doctor 4–6 weeks before travel for vaccinations and advice
Keep hand sanitizer and masks handy, especially on trains and crowded areas
Recommended vaccinations include Hepatitis A, Hepatitis B, Typhoid, and routine vaccines (MMR, DPT, influenza)

health_tips.tip: Japanese Encephalitis sometimes advised for outdoor rural trips, but not necessary for city-based itinerary

health_tips.tip: Carry copy of doctor's prescription for prescription medications

health_tips.tip: Japan restricts some over-the-counter medications like those with pseudoephedrine - carry only necessary quantities and check with airline/customs if unsure

health_tips.tip: Travel Insurance- Recommendation- TATA AIG. Doctors prescriptions/travel prescriptions for medicines (except over the counter meds)
Carry insect repellent for rural areas or parks
Tap water is safe in Japan; eat freshly prepared food, especially sushi or street snacks
Travel insurance strongly recommended — includes medical coverage and emergencies
Bring necessary prescriptions and a copy of the prescription
Use sunscreen and hats for day trips, especially in April
Carry a digital or printed health record for emergencies
Keep hand sanitizer and masks handy, especially on trains and crowded areas
No mandatory vaccinations are required for entry into Japan
Recommended vaccines include routine shots: MMR (measles, mumps, rubella), DTP (diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis), polio, and influenza
Seasonal flu vaccine is advisable if traveling in autumn or winter (Nov–Feb)
Carry personal medications in original packaging, with a doctor's prescription
Declare medicines if they contain controlled substances (especially narcotics or psychotropics)
Tap water is safe to drink across Japan
Travel insurance with medical coverage is strongly recommended
In case of illness, visit hospitals with 'International Clinics' for English-speaking doctors
Check food ingredients – Vegetarian items may contain fish stock (dashi)
Use ATMs at convenience stores – Most foreign cards work there
Carry hand sanitizer and tissues – handy for public transport
Mosquito protection: Carry insect repellent for rural areas or parks
Food & Water Safety: Tap water is safe in Japan; eat freshly prepared food, especially sushi or street snacks
Travel Insurance: Strongly recommended — includes medical coverage and emergencies
Allergies / Medications: Bring necessary prescriptions and a copy of the prescription
Sun Protection: Use sunscreen and hats for day trips, especially in April
Japan has modern hospitals and clinics with English-speaking staff in major cities
Pharmacies (薬局 / Yakkyoku) are widely available for over-the-counter medications
For emergencies, dial 119 for ambulance services
Consult your doctor for prescriptions
Carry a digital or printed health record for emergencies
Keep hand sanitizer and masks handy, especially on trains and crowded areas
